summary: dcchbot ships hardcoded author backdoor that exfiltrates installer's Discord bot token and force-updates the running bot
details: The package embeds a hardcoded third-party Discord user ID (CODER_ID = 1317800611441283139, the author's account) alongside the installer-supplied OWNER_ID in authorization checks on privileged slash commands in dcchbot/main.py. Two concrete installer-harm channels result: (1) the `/token` slash command replies with `bot._token` — the Discord bot token the installer supplied at startup — to anyone whose Discord ID matches CODER_ID, giving the author a covert on-demand exfiltration channel for the installer's bot credential; (2) the `/bot-update` command, when invoked by that same hardcoded CODER_ID, executes `os.system(f"pip install dcchbot=={latest_version}")` followed by `os.system("dcchbot")` on the installer's host, letting the author push and auto-execute an arbitrary future version of this package on every deployment. This is not author self-harm: the victim is the operator who runs the bot with their own token. Compounding the risk, `dcchbot/__init__.py` unconditionally calls `run()` on import, so any `import dcchbot` starts an interactive daemon, and `main.py` performs an unconditional import-time `requests.get('https://pypi.org/pypi/dcchbot/json')` that feeds the self-update path. The author-controlled backdoor in privileged command handlers is a deliberate supply-chain attack surface against installers.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
